structured_choices.data_source: Historical maintenance records from manufacturing firm spanning 5 years (60 months). Data includes: equipment identifiers, timestamped usage logs (temperature, load stress, operating hours), repair records (date, type, cost, downtime duration), and failure incidents (date, equipment ID, failure mode). Data sourced from production environment CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System) and operational telemetry systems. Data quality issues: missing temperature readings in early periods (months 1–12, ~15% sparsity); repair logs may be recorded post-hoc (1–3 day delay); some equipment removed/replaced during study period. Mitigation: exclude months 1–12 from analysis window; use recorded repair date as ground truth; stratify by equipment cohort to control for replacement.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary classification: failure event occurs within T days (T=30, aligned with typical maintenance scheduling window). Label is constructed from timestamped failure incident records. For each equipment unit at each time point t, label=1 if a failure is recorded in [t, t+T]; label=0 otherwise. Failures recorded in the CMMS are confirmed by maintenance logs and downtime records, so label noise is minimal. Positive class prevalence is approximately 8–12% across the dataset (consistent with typical manufacturing failure rates).
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with equipment-group stratification. Training: months 13–42 (30 months). Validation: months 43–48 (6 months). Test: months 49–60 (12 months). Justification: RL models require sufficient historical trajectory for learning state-value functions; temporal split prevents future information leakage and mirrors real deployment (model must predict using only past observations). Equipment-group stratification ensures train/val/test contain similar distributions of equipment types, ages, and failure modes; without this, model may overfit to equipment cohorts present in training. Within each split, data is organized as sequences: for each equipment unit, construct rolling windows of [observation history, label] pairs at daily or shift granularity. Leakage prevention: all feature engineering and preprocessing fit only on training data (months 13–42); validation and test use fit parameters from training only.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are fit exclusively on the training split (months 13–42). Scaling parameters (mean, std) for temperature, load stress, and operating hours are computed on training data only. Categorical encoding (equipment type, failure mode vocabulary) is fit on training labels and equipment metadata only. Imputation strategy for missing temperature readings uses forward-fill within equipment sequences (fit on training temporal patterns only). Validation and test sets are transformed using the training-fit parameters without refitting. This prevents leakage of validation/test distribution information into model initialization.
structured_choices.features: Raw signals are organized into three feature groups: (1) Operational state features: rolling statistics (mean, std, max, min) of temperature, load stress, and operating hours computed over windows of 7, 30, and 90 days prior to prediction time t. (2) Maintenance history features: count of repairs in past 30, 90, and 365 days; time since last repair; cumulative maintenance cost in past year; failure mode history (one-hot encoded, fit on training data). (3) Equipment context features: equipment age (months in service), equipment type (categorical, encoded), original equipment manufacturer, and any recorded modifications. All rolling statistics are computed within equipment sequences without cross-equipment leakage. No future information (post-t) is included. Feature dimensionality post-encoding: approximately 40–50 features. Features are designed to capture degradation trends (rolling stats), maintenance activity patterns (repair recency), and static equipment characteristics (type, age).
structured_choices.model: Deep Reinforcement Learning model: a policy network trained to maximize cumulative reward over a maintenance episode. Architecture: state representation is the 40–50 dimensional feature vector at time t; action space is discrete (maintain now / defer maintenance); reward structure penalizes false negatives (unplanned failures) at high cost and penalizes unnecessary maintenance at lower cost, reflecting stakeholder objectives. Specifically: r(t) = +R_prevent if model recommends maintenance and failure occurs within T days; r(t) = –C_unnecessary if model recommends maintenance and no failure occurs; r(t) = –C_miss if model defers and failure occurs; r(t) = 0 if model defers and no failure occurs. R_prevent and C_unnecessary are tuned to reflect business costs (downtime cost >> unnecessary maintenance cost). Use an actor-critic RL algorithm (e.g., PPO or A3C) with experience replay. Training uses on-policy or off-policy trajectories sampled from the training environment (sequences of equipment over months 13–42). Model is trained for a fixed number of episodes or until convergence of validation reward metric.
structured_choices.baseline: Baseline 1 (Statistical Benchmark): Logistic regression with identical feature set (40–50 features, same scaling and encoding, fit on same training data). Logistic regression receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search on validation set (regularization strength L1/L2 balance, threshold optimization). Baseline 2 (Traditional Maintenance Heuristic): Rule-based policy derived from expert maintenance schedules: maintenance is triggered if (time since last repair > 180 days) OR (cumulative maintenance cost in past year > threshold) OR (recent failure count > 1). Heuristic is calibrated using training data to reflect current practice. Both baselines are evaluated on the same validation and test sets using identical metrics. Justification: Logistic regression controls for model complexity and feature engineering effort (same features, both are supervised models for failure prediction); the heuristic baseline represents current practice and is the actual comparison point for stakeholder decision-making.
structured_choices.primary_metric: False Negative Rate (FNR) at the chosen operating point, defined as FNR = FN / (FN + TP). Justification: Stakeholders prioritize minimizing unplanned downtime; a false negative (model predicts no failure but failure occurs) directly leads to unplanned downtime and emergency repairs, which are costly and disruptive. This metric aligns with the business objective of reducing downtime events. Secondary consideration: FNR is evaluated at a threshold chosen to hold precision ≥ 0.70 (to avoid excessive unnecessary maintenance), ensuring both objectives are balanced. If recall-precision trade-off is severe, alternative primary metric: F1 score or cost-weighted metric (cost = FN × cost_downtime + FP × cost_unnecessary_maintenance), weighted by stakeholder-provided costs.
secondary_metrics: True Positive Rate (Sensitivity / Recall): TP / (TP + FN), ensures high sensitivity to actual failures.
Specificity: TN / (TN + FP), measures correctly deferred maintenance.
Precision: TP / (TP + FP), reflects maintenance activity efficiency.
Area Under Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), appropriate for imbalanced classification (8–12% positive class).
Total cost metric: cost = (FN × downtime_cost_per_event) + (FP × maintenance_cost_per_activity), evaluated on test set.
Per-equipment-type performance: stratified metrics to detect if RL model generalizes across different equipment cohorts.
Prediction latency: average inference time, important for real-time deployment.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and model selection are performed exclusively on the validation set (months 43–48). For the RL model: policy network architecture (hidden layer sizes, learning rate, discount factor, entropy regularization), reward weights (R_prevent, C_unnecessary, C_miss), and training episode count are tuned by running multiple training runs on training data and evaluating each on validation data. For logistic regression: regularization strength and decision threshold are tuned via grid search on validation set. Best models (RL and baselines) are selected based on validation FNR at the chosen precision threshold. The test set (months 49–60) is held completely untouched during tuning and is used only for final evaluation to report unbiased performance.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: Test set (months 49–60, held-out 12 months) is used exactly once, after all model selection is complete. No hyperparameters are adjusted based on test results. Final evaluation involves: (1) generating predictions for all test sequences using the selected RL model and baseline models; (2) computing all primary and secondary metrics on test predictions; (3) reporting confidence intervals (via bootstrap or cross-validation within test set) around metrics; (4) performing stratified analysis (by equipment type, age cohorts) to assess generalization. Results are locked and reported; no further tuning is performed.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: variations in manufacturing processes or equipment usage patterns over the 5-year period. Controls: (1) Temporal stratification ensures training (months 13–42), validation (43–48), and test (49–60) are drawn from contiguous periods, reducing the effect of slow process drift. (2) Equipment-group stratification ensures each split contains similar distributions of equipment types and ages. (3) Per-equipment-type performance analysis: secondary metrics computed separately for each major equipment cohort to detect if performance is driven by a subset of equipment or generalizes. (4) Time-of-year / seasonal analysis: check whether validation/test performance varies by season (if applicable) to assess robustness. (5) Sanity check: baseline (logistic regression and heuristic rule) are evaluated on the same train/val/test splits; if baselines also show strong performance, confounds may be driving results rather than model architecture. Scope of claims: This design establishes that RL with the specified feature set and reward structure achieves lower FNR than the statistical and heuristic baselines on this manufacturing firm's data during the test period (months 49–60). It does NOT establish that RL is inherently superior to statistical methods in all manufacturing contexts; generalization to other facilities, equipment types, or time periods requires additional studies or cross-site validation.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Deep RL vs. Traditional Methods for Predictive Maintenance

### 1. Data Source and Labeling

The experiment uses 5 years of historical maintenance records from the manufacturing firm's CMMS and operational telemetry. The analysis window spans months 13–60 (48 months), excluding the first 12 months to avoid data quality issues (15% missing temperature readings early in the dataset). Data quality issues are handled as follows: missing temperature readings are forward-filled within equipment sequences (fit parameters on training data only); repair logs recorded post-hoc (1–3 day delay) use the official repair date as ground truth; equipment replacements are tracked and equipment cohorts are used in stratification.

The outcome is defined as binary: a failure event is labeled if it occurs within T=30 days of the prediction point (a 30-day window aligns with typical maintenance scheduling cycles in manufacturing). Labels are drawn from timestamped failure incident records confirmed by maintenance logs and downtime records, so label noise is minimal. The positive class prevalence is approximately 8–12%, consistent with real manufacturing failure rates.

### 2. Train / Validation / Test Split

A temporal split with equipment-group stratification is used:
- **Training**: Months 13–42 (30 months)
- **Validation**: Months 43–48 (6 months)
- **Test**: Months 49–60 (12 months)

Justification: Temporal splits prevent leakage of future information into the model and reflect real-world deployment scenarios where the model must predict using only past observations. Reinforcement learning models benefit from longer training windows to learn state-value functions over sufficient trajectories. Equipment-group stratification ensures that each split contains a representative mix of equipment types, ages, and failure modes, preventing overfitting to particular equipment cohorts. Within each split, data is organized as rolling-window sequences at daily or shift granularity for each equipment unit.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are constructed in three groups:
1. **Operational state**: Rolling statistics (mean, std, max, min) of temperature, load stress, and operating hours over 7-, 30-, and 90-day windows.
2. **Maintenance history**: Count of repairs in past 30, 90, and 365 days; time since last repair; cumulative maintenance cost in the past year; failure mode history (one-hot encoded).
3. **Equipment context**: Equipment age, equipment type (categorical), manufacturer, and recorded modifications.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, categorical encoding) are fit exclusively on the training split (months 13–42). Scaling parameters are computed on training data; categorical vocabularies are built from training labels and metadata only. Validation and test sets are transformed using training-fit parameters without refitting. This prevents leakage of validation/test distribution information into model initialization. Rolling statistics are computed within equipment sequences with no cross-equipment leakage, and no future information (post-prediction point) is included.

### 4. Model, Baseline, and Tuning

The **primary model** is a deep reinforcement learning policy network trained to maximize cumulative reward over maintenance episodes. State representation is the 40–50 dimensional feature vector; action space is discrete (maintain / defer); reward structure penalizes false negatives (high cost) and unnecessary maintenance (lower cost) to reflect stakeholder objectives.

Two **baselines** receive equivalent tuning effort:
1. **Logistic Regression**: Identical feature set (40–50 features), same scaling/encoding, fit on training data. Hyperparameters (regularization strength, decision threshold) are tuned via grid search on the validation set to optimize FNR at precision ≥ 0.70.
2. **Heuristic Rule**: Expert-derived maintenance triggers (time since last repair, cumulative cost, recent failure count), calibrated on training data to reflect current practice.

Hyperparameter tuning for the RL model (policy architecture, learning rate, reward weights, episode count) is performed by running multiple training runs on training data and evaluating on the validation set. Best models are selected based on validation FNR; the test set is never used for tuning.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: False Negative Rate (FNR = FN / (FN + TP)), evaluated at a threshold chosen to maintain precision ≥ 0.70. FNR directly aligns with the stakeholder objective of minimizing unplanned downtime: false negatives lead to emergency repairs and costly production interruptions. This metric is appropriate for the imbalanced classification setting (8–12% positive class).

**Secondary metrics** include: Recall, Specificity, Precision, Area Under Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), total cost (FN × downtime cost + FP × maintenance cost), per-equipment-type stratified performance, and prediction latency. Cost-weighted metrics reflect business objectives; stratified performance checks generalization across equipment cohorts.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set (months 43–48). The test set (months 49–60) is held completely untouched during model selection and is used exactly once for final unbiased evaluation. After model selection, predictions are generated for all test sequences; primary and secondary metrics are computed; confidence intervals are estimated via bootstrap; and stratified analyses are performed by equipment type and age cohort. Results are locked and reported without further tuning.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

The primary confound is variation in manufacturing processes or equipment usage patterns over the 5-year period. Controls include: (1) temporal stratification to reduce process drift effects; (2) equipment-group stratification to ensure balanced equipment distributions; (3) per-equipment-type performance analysis to detect cohort-specific effects; (4) seasonal analysis if applicable. Baseline models (logistic regression and heuristic rule) are evaluated on identical splits; if baselines also perform strongly, confounds may dominate results rather than model architecture.

**Scope of claims**: The design establishes that RL with the specified features and reward structure achieves lower FNR than statistical and heuristic baselines on this firm's data during the test period. Generalization to other facilities, equipment types, or time horizons requires additional studies.
